content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 7, 1993

Terry Majuk
California Association of
Hospitals and Health Systems
1201 K Street, Suite 800
Post Office Box 1100
Sacramento, CA 95812-1100

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-435

Dear Ms. Majuk:

You have requested advice regarding the gift provisions of
the Political Reform Act.¹

QUESTION

Will the free use of the California Association of Hospitals
and Health Systems' ("CAHHS") conference center by a legislator or
a state agency official constitute a gift to these individuals?

CONCLUSION

The free use of the CAHHS' conference center will be a gift
to the legislator or state official who requests its use unless,
in offering use of the conference center, CAHHS satisfies the
following criteria:

1. CAHHS intends to donate use of the conference center to
the Legislature or state agency and not to the specific requesting
official;

2. The Legislature or state agency exercises substantial
control over the use of the gift;

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-93-435
Page 2


     3.  CAHHS has not limited use of the gift to specified or
high level employees, but rather has made it generally available
to agency personnel in connection with agency business without
regard to official status; and

     4.  The agency (including the Legislature) receiving free use
of the conference center formalizes the making and use of the gift
in a resolution (or written public record in the case of
administrative agencies not possessing the legislative power of
adopting resolutions) which embodies the standards set forth
above.2
                              FACTS

     CAHHS has developed a conference center located in the 1201 K
Street Building on the K Street Mall in Sacramento.  The center
includes one room which seats up to 250 persons and one room that
seats up to 30 persons.  These two rooms are available to be
rented at a fixed rate.

     You have been asked by a legislative committee consultant and
a state agency deputy director to provide the use of a conference
center room, free of charge, for meetings.  You have asked whether
the free use of a conference room would constitute a gift to these
individuals.

                             ANALYSIS

     The Act defines the term "gift" as:

     ... any payment to the extent that consideration of equal or
greater value is not received....

                              Section 82028.

     The term "payment" is defined in Section 82044 to include:

     ... a payment, distribution, transfer, loan advance, deposit,
gift or other rendering of money, property, services or
anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible.

     However, in In re Stone (1977) 3 FPPC Opinions 52, the
Commission determined that there may be some situations where
surrounding circumstances show that the gift was made to the
official's agency only, without providing any significant or
unusual benefit to the official.  To determine that a gift has
been made to the official's agency and not to the official who
uses it, the following four criteria must be satisfied:

_______________________________
     2  The Commission is currently considering adoption of a
regulation on this specific subject so these criteria may change.
Enclosed is the most recent draft of the regulation, which will be
considered for adoption at the Commission's January 6, 1994,
meeting.  Please contact us after that date if you desire to know
the status of the regulation.

Our File No. A-93-435
Page 3


     1.     The donor intends to donate the gift to the agency and
not to the official;

     2.     The agency exercises substantial control over use of the
gift;

     3.     The donor has not limited use of the gift to specified
or high level employees, but rather has made it generally
available to agency personnel in connection with agency business
without regard to official status; and

     4.     The making and use of the gift is formalized in a
resolution (or written public record in the case of
administrative agencies not possessing the legislative power
of adopting resolutions) which embodies the standards set
forth above.

                              3 FPPC Opinions at 57.

     The Stone opinion concluded that officials who receive use of
a gift which satisfies the above standards need not disclose the
gift.

     As of the date of this letter, the Commission's Stone Opinion
will apply to your question.  Compliance with the criteria set
forth in that opinion may permit you to allow an agency or the
Legislature to use your facilities without making a gift to a
public official.  As explained above (see footnote 2), the
Commission is considering a regulation on this subject and may
adopt the regulation at its meeting on January 6, 1994.  When
adopted, this regulation will codify the criteria a donor must
follow under these circumstances.

     If you have additional questions, please do not hesitate to
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                              Sincerely,

                              Steven G. Churchwell
                              General Counsel

                              [signature]

                              By:  Lynda Doherty
                                   Political Reform Consultant

SGC:ld

Enclosure
